mission.title: Install and Configure Kube Ovn on Kubernetes
mission.description: Kube-OVN integrates OVN-based Network Virtualization with Kubernetes, providing enhanced networking capabilities such as multi-tenancy, VPC support, and seamless VM live migration. Installing Kube-OVN allows you to leverage advanced networking features in your Kubernetes cluster.

steps.title: Add the Kube-OVN Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the official Kube-OVN Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration with the following command:
```bash
helm repo add kube-ovn https://skalanetworks.github.io/charts
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Kube-OVN using Helm
steps.description:
Install Kube-OVN in the kube-system namespace with the specified version:
```bash
helm install kube-ovn kube-ovn/kube-ovn-v2 --namespace kube-system --create-namespace --version 1.16.0
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check the status of the Kube-OVN pods to ensure they are running:
```bash
kubectl get pods -n kube-system
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits
steps.description:
Edit the Kube-OVN deployment to set resource limits for the pods:
```bash
kubectl edit deployment kube-ovn -n kube-system
``` 
Add the following under the containers section:
```yaml
resources:
  limits:
    cpu: "500m"
    memory: "512Mi"
  requests:
    cpu: "250m"
    memory: "256Mi"
```

steps.title: Set up monitoring with Prometheus
steps.description:
Enable Prometheus monitoring by configuring the necessary annotations in the Kube-OVN deployment:
```bash
kubectl annotate deployment kube-ovn -n kube-system prometheus.io/scrape=true
kubectl annotate deployment kube-ovn -n kube-system prometheus.io/port=9100
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation will have Kube-OVN pods running in the kube-system namespace, and you will be able to verify the deployment and resource limits. Additionally, Prometheus monitoring will be set up for Kube-OVN.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add kube-ovn https://skalanetworks.github.io/charts
helm repo update

resources:
  limits:
    cpu: "500m"
    memory: "512Mi"
  requests:
    cpu: "250m"
    memory: "256Mi"

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the Kube-OVN release from the kube-system namespace using Helm. This will remove the deployment and associated resources managed by Helm.
```bash
helm uninstall kube-ovn --namespace kube-system
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
Remove any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent volume claims (PVCs) associated with Kube-OVN to ensure a clean uninstallation.
```bash
kubectl delete crd -l app=kube-ovn
kubectl delete pvc -l app=kube-ovn -n kube-system
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Delete the kube-system namespace if it is no longer needed, and verify that all resources have been removed.
```bash
kubectl delete namespace kube-system
kubectl get all -n kube-system
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, back up the current state of the Kube-OVN installation to prevent data loss. You can export the current configuration.
```bash
kubectl get all -n kube-system -o yaml > kube-ovn-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository and upgrade the Kube-OVN release to the latest version. Replace `1.16.1` with the desired version.
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade kube-ovn kube-ovn/kube-ovn-v2 --namespace kube-system --version 1.16.1
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
After the upgrade, check the status of the Kube-OVN pods to ensure they are running the new version.
```bash
kubectl get pods -n kube-system
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If Kube-OVN pods are in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs for errors that might indicate the cause of the failure.
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> -n kube-system
```

troubleshooting.title: Network connectivity issues
troubleshooting.description:
If you experience network connectivity issues, verify that the Kube-OVN components are running and check the network policies.
```bash
kubectl get pods -n kube-system
kubectl get networkpolicy -n kube-system
```

troubleshooting.title: Resource limits causing OOMKilled
troubleshooting.description:
If pods are being killed due to out-of-memory (OOM) errors, review the resource limits set for Kube-OVN and adjust them as necessary.
```bash
kubectl describe pod <pod-name> -n kube-system | grep -i 'oom'
```

troubleshooting.title: Prometheus metrics not scraping
troubleshooting.description:
If Prometheus is not scraping metrics from Kube-OVN, check the annotations and ensure that Prometheus is configured to scrape the correct endpoints.
```bash
kubectl get deployment kube-ovn -n kube-system -o yaml | grep annotations -A 5
```

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running version 1.24 or higher, and have Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine.
